behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure — generating a generic reply that ignores the email's content — only manifests when a real LLM processes the actual email thread. LLM-DEP: A stub always returns the same pre-programmed response, so a test with a stub never verifies that the LLM actually grounds its reply in the incoming email's specifics. Whether the model references particular points depends on the model's actual behavior and the prompt design, not on deterministic code logic. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the LLM would make this test trivially pass (the stub always 'references' whatever you hard-code), permanently hiding prompt-design or context-window failures. REJECTION-GATE: Integration is disqualified because no amount of in-process module wiring can substitute for observing what the real model actually generates in response to a real email.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real incoming email thread with 3–4 distinct named claims or questions into the drafting pipeline with a live LLM call. Assert that the generated draft explicitly addresses at least those named claims or questions by keyword or semantic match, and does not return a boilerplate reply lacking those specifics.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: A factual consistency checker in an LLM-based assistant is itself typically LLM-driven (semantic entailment or contradiction detection), meaning its correctness depends on the model's actual classification decisions. MOCK-HIDE: Stubbing the checker always returns a fixed 'flagged/not-flagged' result, hiding failures where the real model misses subtle contradictions or is confused by paraphrase. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: Even if the checker were implemented as deterministic string-matching, the behavior asserts semantic contradiction detection — the kind of match that varies across KB phrasing, paraphrase, and negation, and that only a real ML/LLM call can evaluate faithfully. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would be sufficient only if the checker were a deterministic lookup — nothing in the behavior description disqualifies an LLM-backed semantic contradiction detector.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a draft that contains at least two claims: one that directly contradicts a KB entry and one that agrees with a KB entry. Run the real consistency-check step against the real KB. Assert the contradicting claim is flagged and the agreeing claim is not flagged.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Whether the LLM includes a disclaimer when it receives no KB context is entirely a function of the model's instruction-following and behavior — it is an LLM steering test. MOCK-HIDE: A stub always returns what you hard-code; testing with a stub merely confirms the stub respects the disclaimer, not that the real model does. MOCK-MISS: The failure mode (model fabricates rather than disclaims) is invisible without a real model call, because the stub never hallucinates. REJECTION-GATE: Integration is disqualified because an in-process fake for the LLM cannot reproduce the real model's tendency to confabulate facts when context is absent — that is precisely what the behavior is designed to detect.
behaviors.test_description: Supply an empty KB (zero relevant entries returned) and a real email thread asking a factual question. Run the draft generation step with a real LLM call. Assert the resulting draft contains a disclaimer phrase (e.g., 'I don't have information on…' or 'I cannot confirm…') and does not assert the queried fact as true.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure — accidental auto-send — only surfaces when the approval gate module and the email-send module are wired together; a unit test of the gate function in isolation cannot confirm the send path is actually gated. LLM-DEP: The approval gate is pure control-flow logic — whether a send is permitted depends on a user-provided boolean state, not on any LLM decision. No real model output is needed. REAL-INFRA: The behavior is about blocking, not actual mail delivery; an in-process stub for the email sender is sufficient to verify the send function is never invoked without approval. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network required — a spy/stub on the send module suffices. (b) No AI/ML output involved. (c) No chained agentic steps. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic is disqualified because the correctness of the approval gate does not depend on what a model returns.
behaviors.test_description: Wire together the draft-presenter module and the email-send module with a spy replacing the actual send transport. (1) Trigger the pipeline up to the approval-presentation step without providing user approval — assert the spy was never called. (2) Provide explicit user approval — assert the spy is called exactly once. Confirm no timing or background task bypasses the gate.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly names a sequence of four distinct stages, at least two of which (generate draft, check facts) involve real LLM/ML model decisions whose outputs govern the next stage's input. MOCK-HIDE: Stubbing any stage breaks the chain: a stubbed generator hides prompt/context failures; a stubbed checker hides consistency-detection failures; context or state lost between stages (e.g., KB context not carried into the consistency check) is invisible unless all stages are real. LLM-DEP: Both generation and consistency-checking depend on real model output, so the minimum for each individually is Agentic — sequencing them elevates to Workflow. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic (single step) is disqualified by the explicit multi-step chain enumerated in the behavior description. Integration is disqualified because at least two stages depend on real LLM output.
behaviors.test_description: Run the complete pipeline end-to-end against a real email thread using live LLM calls: (1) read and parse the thread, (2) generate a draft, (3) run the factual consistency check against a real KB with at least one contradicting entry, (4) present for approval. Assert: draft is non-empty and addresses thread content, the contradicting claim is flagged in the consistency report, the approval gate holds (send spy not invoked), and the system surfaces the draft for user review.
